topology: relay=node-1 (cgroup 2vCPU/4G, advertise 172.31.43.122), responder=node-2 (172.31.19.13), generator=node-3 (172.31.88.18). All same VPC, different AZs (1c/1b/1a). Real cross-VPC network between every hop. SG: all-from-self live.

matchmaking_same_vpc.finding: Same-VPC peers hole-punch to direct 20/20 — the relay carries NOTHING after handoff. This is the cheap side of the matchmaking-vs-passthrough gap: where hole-punch succeeds, relay steady-state cost per pair is ~0. The expensive side (hole-punch FAILS -> matchmaking falls back to relay) needs a genuinely NAT'd client (node-4 Mac + public ingress on 3343/3478) and is NOT yet measured.

passthrough_throughput.comment_forwarded: each connection: gen sends 4MiB -> relay -> responder, responder echoes 4MiB -> relay -> gen; relay forwards ~8MiB/conn x 50
passthrough_throughput.finding: Relay forwards ~93 MiB per CPU-second => ~186 MiB/s CPU-bound on the 2-vCPU slice. At this scale the relay passthrough wall is CPU, not NIC (loopback-free, real cross-VPC). Wall MiB/s (73) is lower because it includes connection establishment + 50 concurrent transfers; the CPU-normalized number is the robust one. First-order; a sustained-transfer mode would refine it.

caveats: Throughput run conflates establishment CPU with forwarding CPU in the window; treat ~93 MiB/cpu-s as first-order.

Generators are box-class (2 vCPU), not larger-than-relay, so a single generator host can itself bottleneck before the relay does at higher scale — distribute generators (node-2/node-3 + Mac) for the true ceiling.
The matchmaking hole-punch-FAILS case (the expensive half of the cost gap) requires the NAT'd Mac + public ingress and is still open.
